subject: Chemistry
q: In an ice crystal, each water molecule is hydrogen bonded to _____ neighbouring molecules.
question: In an ice crystal, each water molecule is hydrogen bonded to _____ neighbouring molecules.
solution:
Hydrogen bonding occurs between hydrogen and strongly electronegative atoms like F, N, O. In water or ice hydrogen bonding occurs as there is hydrogen as well as oxygen both are present. Water has 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom. The oxygen of one water molecule has two lone pairs of electrons, each of which can form hydrogen bonds with hydrogen on other two water molecules.

Each water molecule is H-bonded to 4 neighbouring molecules.

options: O22-<O2-<O2<O2+
solution:
Bond order =number of bonding e--number of antibonding e-2

Electronic configuration of O2, O2+, O2- and O2-2 are:

O2=σ1s2σ1s2*σ2s2σ2s2*σ2px2π2py2=π2pz2π2py1*=π2pz1* (Bond order=2)O2+=σ1s2σ1s2*σ2s2σ2s2*σ2px2π2py2=π2pz2π2py1*=π2pz0* (Bond order=2.5)O2-=σ1s2σ1s2*σ2s2σ2s2*σ2px2π2py2=π2pz2π2py2*=π2pz1* (Bond order=1.5)O2-2=σ1s2σ1s2*σ2s2σ2s2*σ2px2π2py2=π2pz2π2py2*=π2pz2* (Bond order=1)

question: The dipole moments of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msub></math> are in the order
solution:
In CHCl3, dipole moment of C-H is added to the resultant dipole moment of three C-Cl bonds, hence compound has non-zero value of dipole moment.

Electronegativity of carbon =2.5
Electronegativity of hydrogen=2.1.


Elelctrongegativity of C=2.5
Electronegativity of Cl=3

Due to symmetry and similar bonds, vector summation of net dipole moments of both CH4, CCl4 are zero, hence equal.

q:
Match List - I with List - II according to shape.
\(\begin{array}{llll} & \text{List-I} & & \text{List-II} \\ A. & \mathrm{XeO_3} & I. & \mathrm{BrF_5} \\ B. & \mathrm{XeF_2} & II. & \mathrm{NH_3} \\ C. & \mathrm{XeO_2F_2} & III. & [\mathrm{I_3}]^- \\ D. & \mathrm{XeOF_4} & IV. & \mathrm{SF_4} \end{array}\)
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Match List - I with List - II according to shape.
\(\begin{array}{llll} & \text{List-I} & & \text{List-II} \\ A. & \mathrm{XeO_3} & I. & \mathrm{BrF_5} \\ B. & \mathrm{XeF_2} & II. & \mathrm{NH_3} \\ C. & \mathrm{XeO_2F_2} & III. & [\mathrm{I_3}]^- \\ D. & \mathrm{XeOF_4} & IV. & \mathrm{SF_4} \end{array}\)
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
Determine molecular shapes using VSEPR theory:
A. XeO₃ (3 bonds, 2 lone pairs on Xe): pyramidal
B. XeF₂ (2 bonds, 3 lone pairs on Xe): linear
C. XeO₂F₂ (4 bonds, 2 lone pairs on Xe): see-saw
D. XeOF₄ (5 bonds, 1 lone pair on Xe): square pyramidal
Matching with shapes:
I. BrF₅: square pyramidal → D-I
II. NH₃: pyramidal → A-II
III. [I₃]⁻: linear → B-III
IV. SF₄: see-saw → C-IV

subject: Chemistry
q:
Which statements are NOT TRUE about $\mathrm{XeO}_{2} \mathrm{~F}_{2}$ ?
A. It has a see-saw shape.
B. Xe has 5 electron pairs in its valence shell in $\mathrm{XeO}_{2} \mathrm{~F}_{2}$.
C. The $\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{Xe}-\mathrm{O}$ bond angle is close to $180^{\circ}$.
D. The $\mathrm{F}-\mathrm{Xe}-\mathrm{F}$ bond angle is close to $180^{\circ}$.
E. Xe has 16 valence electrons in $\mathrm{XeO}_{2} \mathrm{~F}_{2}$.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Which statements are NOT TRUE about $\mathrm{XeO}_{2} \mathrm{~F}_{2}$ ?
A. It has a see-saw shape.
B. Xe has 5 electron pairs in its valence shell in $\mathrm{XeO}_{2} \mathrm{~F}_{2}$.
C. The $\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{Xe}-\mathrm{O}$ bond angle is close to $180^{\circ}$.
D. The $\mathrm{F}-\mathrm{Xe}-\mathrm{F}$ bond angle is close to $180^{\circ}$.
E. Xe has 16 valence electrons in $\mathrm{XeO}_{2} \mathrm{~F}_{2}$.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
In XeO$_2$F$_2$: Xe has 8 valence electrons forming 4 bonding pairs (2 with O, 2 with F) and 2 lone pairs, giving a trigonal bipyramidal electron geometry with a see-saw molecular shape, making statement A TRUE.
Statement B is NOT TRUE because the total electron pairs around Xe is 6 (4 bonding + 2 lone), not 5.
For statement C, the O-Xe-O angle in a see-saw geometry is approximately 120°, not close to 180°, making it NOT TRUE.
Statement D is TRUE as the F-Xe-F angle is approximately 180° when F atoms occupy axial positions.
Statement E is NOT TRUE since Xe has only 8 valence electrons, not 16.
The incorrect statements are B, C, and E.

q:
Arrange the following in the decreasing order of their covalent character :

(A) LiCl

(B) NaCl

(C) KCl

(D) CsCl

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below
question:
Arrange the following in the decreasing order of their covalent character :

(A) LiCl

(B) NaCl

(C) KCl

(D) CsCl

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below
solution:
Lithium chloride is the alkali metal chloride which is most covalent in nature. This is due to the small size and high polarising power of lithium. That is, smaller the cation, greater is the polarising power and hence, greater is the covalent character.

So order of covalent character will be CsCl<RbCl<NaCl<LiCl

q:
Among the following molecules/ ions, 

C22-, N22-,O22-, O2

Which one is diamagnetic and has the shortest bond length?
question:
Among the following molecules/ ions, 

C22-, N22-,O22-, O2

Which one is diamagnetic and has the shortest bond length?
solution:
O2(16)=    σ1s2σ*1s2 σ2s2 σ*2s2 σ2pz2 π2px2=π2py2 π* 2px1=π2py1

Paramagnetic; Bond order = 2

O22-(18)=    σ1s2σ*1s2 σ2s2 σ*2s2 σ2pz2  π2px2=π2py2 π* 2px2=π*2py2

Diamagnetic; Bond order = 1

N22-(16)=    σ1s2σ*1s2 σ 2s2 σ*2s2 π2px2 π2py2 σ2pz2π*2px1 =π*2py1

Paramagnetic; Bond order = 2

 C22-(14)=    σ1s2σ*1s2 σ 2s2 σ*2s2 π2px2=π2py2 σ2pz2

Diamagnetic; Bond order =3

Bond order ∝1Bond length

Hence C22 is diamagnetic and is having shortest bond length.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements :
Statement (I): Experimentally determined oxygen-oxygen bond lengths in the $\mathrm{O}_3$ are found to be same and the bond length is greater than that of a $\mathrm{O}=\mathrm{O}$ (double bond) but less than that of a single $(O-O)$ bond.
Statement (II) : The strong lone pair-lone pair repulsion between oxygen atoms is solely responsible for the fact that the bond length in ozone is smaller than that of a double bond $(\mathrm{O}=\mathrm{O})$ but more than that of a single bond $(\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{O})$.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements :
Statement (I): Experimentally determined oxygen-oxygen bond lengths in the $\mathrm{O}_3$ are found to be same and the bond length is greater than that of a $\mathrm{O}=\mathrm{O}$ (double bond) but less than that of a single $(O-O)$ bond.
Statement (II) : The strong lone pair-lone pair repulsion between oxygen atoms is solely responsible for the fact that the bond length in ozone is smaller than that of a double bond $(\mathrm{O}=\mathrm{O})$ but more than that of a single bond $(\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{O})$.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
solution: Bond length $\propto \frac{1}{\text { Bond order }}$ Order of $\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{O}$ bond length $\mathrm{O}=\mathrm{O} < \mathrm{O}_3 < \mathrm{O}-\mathrm{O}$ $\therefore$ Statement $I$ is true. Lone pair-lone pair repulsion between O-atoms is not solely responsible for the correct order of O-O bond length. Bond order also should be considered. $\therefore \quad$ Statement II is false.

q: The number of species below that have two lone pairs of electrons in their central atom is ___ (Round off to the Nearest integer) <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>SF</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msubsup><mi>BF</mi><mn>4</mn><mo>-</mo></msubsup><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>ClF</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>AsF</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>PCl</mi><mn>5</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>BrF</mi><mn>5</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>XeF</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>SF</mi><mn>6</mn></msub></math>
question: The number of species below that have two lone pairs of electrons in their central atom is ___ (Round off to the Nearest integer) <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>SF</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msubsup><mi>BF</mi><mn>4</mn><mo>-</mo></msubsup><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>ClF</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>AsF</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>PCl</mi><mn>5</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>BrF</mi><mn>5</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>XeF</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>SF</mi><mn>6</mn></msub></math>
solution:
Two l.p. on central atom is =ClF3,XeF4

The valence shell electron pair repulsion (VSEPR) theory is a model used to predict 3-D molecular geometry based on the number of valence shell electron bond pairs among the atoms in a molecule or ion. This model assumes that electron pairs will arrange themselves to minimize repulsion effects from one another.

q: If the magnetic moment of a di-oxygen species is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1.73 </mn><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>.</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo>.</mo></math>, it may be
question: If the magnetic moment of a di-oxygen species is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1.73 </mn><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>.</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo>.</mo></math>, it may be
solution:
Given, magnetic moment of di-oxygen species=1.73 B.M.

We know, magnetic moment = n n+2 , where n=number of unpaired electrons

1.73= n n+2

n=1
So, species should contain one unpaired electron.

Let us check by electronic configuration of each species

O2=σ1s2 σ*1s2 σ2s2 σ*2s2 σ2p2z π2p2x=π2p2y π*2p1x=π*2p1yO2+=σ1s2 σ*1s2 σ2s2 σ*2s2 σ2p2z π2p2x=π2p2y π*2p1x=π*2p0yO2-=σ1s2 σ*1s2 σ2s2 σ*2s2 σ2p2z π2p2x=π2p2y π*2p2x=π*2p1y
 

As clear by above electronic configurations that, O2 has two unpaired electrons and O2- & O2+  Each has only one unpaired electron; hence magnetic moment of  O2+ & O2- is 1.73 B.M.

subject: Chemistry
q:
The number of species having a square planar shape from the following is

XeF4, SF4, SiF4, BF4-, BrF4-, CuNH342+, FeCl42-, PtCl42-
question:
The number of species having a square planar shape from the following is

XeF4, SF4, SiF4, BF4-, BrF4-, CuNH342+, FeCl42-, PtCl42-
solution:
XeF4 is sp3d2 hybridised with two lone pairs of electrons, hence, it is square planar.

SF4 is sp3d hybridised with one lone pairs of electrons, hence, it is See saw.

SiF4 is sp3 hybridised with zero lone pairs of electrons, hence, it is Tetrahedral

BF4- is sp3 hybridised with zero lone pairs of electrons, hence, it is Tetrahedral

[Cu(NH3)4] 2+ is dsp2 hybridised and is Square planar

[FeCl4] 2– is sp3 hybridised and is Tetrahedral

[PtCl4] 2– is dsp2 hybridised and is Square planar

BrF4-  is sp3d2 with  Square planar geometry.

So, 4 square planer shape compounds are present.

q: The number of bent-shaped molecule/s from the following is ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msubsup><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mn>3</mn><mo>-</mo></msubsup><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msubsup><mi>NO</mi><mn>2</mn><mo>-</mo></msubsup><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msubsup><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mn>3</mn><mo>-</mo></msubsup><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>SO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>
question: The number of bent-shaped molecule/s from the following is ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msubsup><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mn>3</mn><mo>-</mo></msubsup><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msubsup><mi>NO</mi><mn>2</mn><mo>-</mo></msubsup><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msubsup><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mn>3</mn><mo>-</mo></msubsup><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>SO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>
solution:
N3-Azide: N¯=N+=N¯↔N¯-N+≡N

It has sp hybridised central atom. Hence it is linear in shape.

I3-(triiodide):

                          

It has linear geometry, sp3d hybridisation with three lone pairs at central atom.

NO2- (nitrite) :  

It is (nonlinear) bent shaped as it has sp2 hybridisation with one lone pair at central atom.

O3 (ozone) :  

It Is bent in shape with sp2 hybridisation and one lone pair at central atom.

SO2(sulphur dioxide) : 

                    

It is bent in shape with sp2 hybridisation and one lone pair at central atom.

So 3 among the given molecules is bent in shape.

subject: Chemistry
q: Which one of the following does not have a pyramidal shape?
question: Which one of the following does not have a pyramidal shape?
solution: In case of $\mathrm{N}\left(\mathrm{SiH}_3\right)_3 \mathrm{~N}$ atom is $s p^2$ hybridised, the lone pair is present in $2 p$ orbital and it is transferred to empty $d$ orbital of Si forming $d \pi-p \pi$ bond. Hence nitrogen with $s p^2$ hybridization has trigonal planar shape.

subject: Chemistry
q: According to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>MO</mi></math> theory the bond orders for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>-</mo></mrow></msup></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>CO</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>NO</mi><mo>+</mo></msup></math> respectively, are
question: According to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>MO</mi></math> theory the bond orders for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>-</mo></mrow></msup></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>CO</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>NO</mi><mo>+</mo></msup></math> respectively, are
solution:
(i) Molecular orbital configuration of O22- is KK (σ2s)2 (σ*2s)2 𝜋(2px)2 𝜋(2py)2 (σ2pz)2 𝜋*(2px)2 𝜋*(2py)2

Bond order = 12 (Nb - Na) = 12 8 - 6 = 1

(ii) Molecular orbital configuration of CO is KK (σ2s)2 (σ*2s)2 𝜋(2px)2 𝜋(2py)2 (σ2pz)2 

Bond order = 12 (Nb - Na) = 12 8 - 2 = 3

(iii) Molecular orbital configuration of NO+ is KK (σ2s)2 (σ*2s)2 𝜋(2px)2 𝜋(2py)2 (σ2pz)2 

Bond order = 12 (Nb - Na) = 12 8 - 2 = 3

So, according to molecular orbital theory, the bond orders respectively are 1, 3 and 3.

subject: Chemistry
q: The total number of anti bonding molecular orbitals, formed from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>s</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>p</mi></math> atomic orbitals in a diatomic molecule is _____________.
question: The total number of anti bonding molecular orbitals, formed from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>s</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>p</mi></math> atomic orbitals in a diatomic molecule is _____________.
solution:
The atomic orbitals combine to form a new set of equivalent orbitals known as hybrid orbitals. Unlike pure orbitals, hybrid orbitals are used in bond formation. The phenomenon is known as hybridisation, which can be defined as the process of intermixing of the orbitals of slightly different energies to redistribute their energies, resulting in the formation of a new set of orbitals of equivalent energies and shape.

The number of hybrid orbitals is equal to the number of atomic orbitals that get hybridised.

Antibonding molecular orbital from 2s=1 that is σ*2s

Antibonding molecular orbital from 2p=3 that is σ*2px,π*2py and π*2pz.

Total =4

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements :
Statement (I) : A π bonding MO has lower electron density above and below the inter-nuclear axis.

Statement (II) : The π* antibonding MO has a node between the nuclei.

In light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements :
Statement (I) : A π bonding MO has lower electron density above and below the inter-nuclear axis.

Statement (II) : The π* antibonding MO has a node between the nuclei.

In light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
solution:
The pi bonding molecular orbital has a larger electron density above and below the internuclear axis due to the symmetry of the orbital, interference of atomic orbitals, and overlap of atomic orbitals. The electron density is minimum along the internuclear axis due to the nodal plane.

A π bonding molecular orbital has higher electron density above and below inter nuclear axis.

This π* orbital has two nodes: one node is the plane which contains the atoms, and the other node is a plane perpendicular to this, between the two carbon atoms.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R :

Assertion A : The H-O-H bond angle in water molecule is 104.5°.

Reason R : The lone pair - lone pair repulsion of electrons is higher than the bond pair - bond pair repulsion.
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R :

Assertion A : The H-O-H bond angle in water molecule is 104.5°.

Reason R : The lone pair - lone pair repulsion of electrons is higher than the bond pair - bond pair repulsion.
options: A is false but R is true
Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
solution:
H2O

θ=104.5°

The hybridisation of oxygen is water molecule is sp3.

So electron geometry of water molecule is tetrahedral, and the bond angle should be 109°28'' but as we know that lone pair-lone pair repulsion of electrons is higher than the bond pair-bond pair repulsion because lone pair occupies more space around central atom than that of bond pair, The H-O-H bond angle in water molecule is 104.5°.

q: The compound of xenon with zero dipole moment is
question: The compound of xenon with zero dipole moment is
solution: $\mathrm{XeF}_4$ has zero dipole moment. It has square planar structure due to which the bond moments of $\mathrm{Xe}-\mathrm{F}$ cancel each other.
